title: Cortex Visualization Suggestions
subtitle: How to make this project understandable at a glance
purpose: Catalogue of concrete visualization ideas for communicating Cortex's architecture, progress, and integration health. Each suggestion names the mochaccino data source that feeds it and the specific question it answers.

suggestions.title: Package Dependency Graph
suggestions.description: Directed acyclic graph of the 4 live packages + 1 planned package. Nodes are packages; edges show workspace dependencies. cortex-contracts is the root with no internal deps. cortex-core and cortex-testkit depend on cortex-contracts. cortex-provider-libsql depends on cortex-contracts. cortex-config (deleted) shows as a ghost node with a merge arrow into cortex-core to document the history. cortex-provider-sqlite-vec (planned) shown as a dashed node.
suggestions.what_it_shows: Dependency direction, which packages can be published independently, and why cortex-contracts must publish first. Makes the 5→4 package merge visible.
suggestions.best_for: Onboarding contributors; explaining publish order; showing what changes break what

suggestions.title: Kernel Method Implementation Matrix
suggestions.description: Grid: rows = kernel methods (27 catalogued), columns = {zone, status, driven_by, KyberBot module adopted}. Status colour: green=implemented, yellow=stubbed, red=missing, grey=deferred. Right panel shows write-parity at 100% and read-parity at 30% as progress bars. The 'contracts-ahead-of-kernel' methods (expiresAt, surprisalScore, lastReinforcedAt) flagged with a distinct colour — they are in schemas but unreachable from any kernel path.
suggestions.what_it_shows: Exactly which kernel methods are real vs stubs, which consumer demanded each one, and the 30% read-parity gap that is the immediate next implementation target.
suggestions.best_for: Sprint planning; communicating integration completeness to KyberBot team

suggestions.title: Contract Entity Relationship Map
suggestions.description: ER diagram of the 10 Zod schemas in cortex-contracts. Entities: Memory, Chunk, Entity, Edge, Fact, Contradiction, Insight, EntityProfile, ProfileEntry, AgentSelf. Relationships: Memory 1→N Chunk; Entity 1→N Edge (source + target); Entity 1→N Fact; Fact 1→N Contradiction (factAId/factBId); EntityProfile contains N ProfileEntry; Insight references Entity; Fact carries optional supersededBy (self-ref). Schema evolution annotations per ADR where applicable (e.g. Memory.status added ADR 007).
suggestions.what_it_shows: The full domain model and how schema evolution across ADRs 004–007 changed each entity. Makes the difference between cortex-contracts (shape) and cortex-core (behaviour) concrete.
suggestions.best_for: Domain understanding; Brain migration planning; KyberBot team reviewing contract changes

suggestions.title: KyberBot Adoption Timeline
suggestions.description: Horizontal swimlane timeline: top lane = Cortex ADRs + kernel method implementations; bottom lane = KyberBot modules closed. Left-to-right is time (2026-05-18 to present). Each Cortex implementation event links to the KyberBot module that demanded it (driven_by field). The 22-commit cortex-adoption branch sits on its own lane. Delivery review result (CONDITIONAL PASS, 2026-05-19) marked as a milestone. 4 remaining KyberBot patches shown as open items after the current end-point.
suggestions.data_source: 01-task-progress.json (v0_x_in_progress tasks, blocked KyberBot patch tasks); 06-kernel-methods.json (adopted_by fields)
suggestions.what_it_shows: The demand-driven development loop in action — how each KyberBot consumer module pulled a kernel method into existence. Demonstrates that Cortex's scope is genuinely demand-driven, not speculative.
suggestions.best_for: Project retrospectives; stakeholder updates; showing integration velocity

suggestions.title: Parity Gap: Cortex vs KyberBot vs Brain
suggestions.description: Three-column matrix where rows are feature areas: [write-path, read-path, sleep-pipeline, vector-search, entity-profile, fact-retrieval, timeline-query, brain-migration, publish]. Columns: {Cortex has it, KyberBot uses it, Brain needs it}. Cell colours: green=done, amber=partial, red=missing, grey=not-applicable. Example cells: write-path row → Cortex green, KyberBot green (22 commits), Brain red (blocked on Convex artifacts). timeline-query → all three red/amber (missing kernel method identified in ADR 008).
suggestions.data_source: 06-kernel-methods.json (summary.write_parity_percent, read_parity_percent, brain_migration_blockers, brain_vs_harness_classification); 01-task-progress.json (todo tasks NS-001 to NS-005)
suggestions.what_it_shows: At a glance where Cortex is complete, where KyberBot has adopted it, and what Brain needs before it can migrate. The 30% read-parity gap and 5 Brain Convex blockers are the two critical gaps.
suggestions.best_for: Architecture reviews; roadmap presentations; deciding what to build next

suggestions.title: Sleep Pipeline Step Diagram
suggestions.description: Linear flow chart of the 13 SLEEP_STEPS (from SLEEP_STEPS const in cortex-core). Each step shown as a box. Steps colour-coded: grey=entirely stubbed (all 13), amber=contracts-ahead-of-kernel (decay scoring, expiry sweep — these touch expiresAt/surprisalScore/lastReinforcedAt fields that exist in FactSchema but are not yet read by any implementation). Step 1 (observe) annotated with KyberBot module #11 (sleep/observe.ts) as the consumer that will eventually demand the implementation. A second swim-lane shows which FactSchema fields each step would consume.
suggestions.what_it_shows: The full shape of the sleep pipeline before it is implemented. Communicates that the contracts are complete but the pipeline is entirely stubbed, and which KyberBot adoption step will demand it.
suggestions.best_for: Planning the sleep implementation sprint; explaining why the schema has fields with no kernel coverage yet

suggestions.title: Provider Landscape: Current + Planned
suggestions.description: Tree rooted at each provider interface (StructuredStore, VectorStore, EmbeddingProvider, LLMProvider, RerankerProvider, Scheduler, JobQueue). Leaf nodes = implementations. Live implementations: cortex-provider-libsql (StructuredStore, green), FakeStructuredStore/FakeVectorStore/FakeEmbeddingProvider/FakeLLMProvider from cortex-testkit (all green, in-memory). KyberBot adapters: OpenAIEmbeddingProvider (EmbeddingProvider), ChromaDBVectorStore (VectorStore) — shown in amber as consumer-side adapters not in Cortex. Planned: cortex-provider-sqlite-vec (VectorStore, grey dashed). cortex-provider-postgres (StructuredStore, grey dashed — needed for Brain migration, not yet built).
suggestions.what_it_shows: Which provider interfaces have real implementations vs only fake/test coverage. Makes it obvious that VectorStore has no production implementation yet (ChromaDB is a consumer adapter, not an Cortex provider). cortex-provider-postgres as a Brain-migration gate.
suggestions.best_for: Architecture documentation; planning the next provider implementation sprint

suggestions.title: Brain Migration Roadmap
suggestions.description: Four columns: {Pre-conditions (Cortex), Pre-conditions (Brain), Migration phases, Post-migration}. Pre-conditions (Cortex): cortex-provider-postgres (not built), getEntityProfile, factRetrieval, timeline query (queryFacts with asOf). Pre-conditions (Brain): shed 5 Convex artifacts — 4-flat-ID edges → NodeRef, shadow entity rows → remove, loose enum vocab → align with ARP scopes, _creationTime → ISO createdAt, reactive query patterns → pull-only. Migration phases: schema alignment, provider swap, Convex artifact removal, kernel wiring. Post-migration: Kyber in Cloud reads and writes through Cortex; Convex retained for non-brain data only.
suggestions.data_source: 01-task-progress.json (v0x.010 ADR 008 note, deferred tasks T18); 06-kernel-methods.json (brain_migration_blockers, brain_vs_harness_classification)
suggestions.what_it_shows: The full dependency chain for Brain migration — what Cortex must build, what Brain must tear down, and in what order. The roadmap makes clear this is post-KyberBot-merge future work.
suggestions.best_for: Brain team planning; Kybernesis roadmap alignment; estimating migration effort

suggestions.title: Publish Pipeline Status Board
suggestions.description: Left-to-right pipeline: [lint → typecheck → test → publish contracts → publish core → publish testkit → publish provider-libsql → git tag v0.1.0]. Each step has a status badge (not_started/wired/green). Current state: all steps not_started or wired; blocked at OTP auth. Annotate that cortex-core now includes merged config loader (so this publish covers what was formerly 5 packages as 4). Secondary lane shows the v0.1.1 CI path (ci.yml → publish.yml idempotency loop) as a future state.
suggestions.what_it_shows: The exact steps needed to unblock npm publish. The 4-package (not 5) count and cortex-core's merged config export are the key post-merge facts to communicate.
suggestions.best_for: Tracking publish readiness; communicating the single manual OTP blocker to stakeholders

suggestions.title: Schema Evolution ADR Trail
suggestions.description: Vertical timeline of ADR events that changed cortex-contracts. Each event: ADR number, date, which schema changed, what was added/modified/removed, and which consumer demand triggered it. ADR 001 (linkNodes rename), ADR 002 (deleteEntity added), ADR 003 (superseded by ADR 004), ADR 004 (FactSchema sentence-form), ADR 005 (updateMemory — Memory not append-only), ADR 006 (markFactSuperseded, storeContradiction, Contradiction.rationale), ADR 007 §3.1 (Memory.status), ADR 007 §3.2 (Memory.isLatest/supersededBy + markMemorySuperseded), ADR 007 §4 (ProfileEntry replaces string[]), ADR 008 (Brain migration analysis). Annotations link to the KyberBot module or architectural audit that drove each change.
suggestions.data_source: 04-contracts-surface.json (evolution arrays per schema + per provider interface); 06-kernel-methods.json (summary.contract_evolutions)
suggestions.what_it_shows: How the contracts have grown from v0.1 initial to current state. Demonstrates that additive-only contract evolution has been maintained throughout. Useful for auditing breaking-vs-additive changes before publish.
suggestions.best_for: Pre-publish contract audit; onboarding new consumers; retrospective on design quality
